skill.name: bioservices
skill.description: Unified Python access to 40+ bioinformatics web services; use when you need to query multiple databases (e.g., UniProt/KEGG/ChEMBL/Reactome) with one consistent API in a single workflow, especially for cross-database analysis and identifier mapping.
skill.summary: Unified Python access to 40+ bioinformatics databases through a single consistent API for cross-database analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 487 static analysis findings evaluated as FALSE POSITIVES. The 350 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are Python/Ruby code blocks in markdown documentation files showing legitimate bioinformatics API usage examples, not actual shell execution. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm', 'C2 keywords', and 'Dynamic function constructor' findings match text in comments, docstrings, and ontology category names (e.g., 'Molecular Function' in GO annotations), not actual executable code patterns. Filesystem and network operations are standard analysis output saving and API endpoint URLs for legitimate biological databases (UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL). This skill is a legitimate open-source bioinformatics library with no malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Documentation code blocks flagged as external command execution
medium_findings.description: 350 findings of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across markdown files. These are Python and Ruby code blocks in markdown documentation demonstrating legitimate bioservices API usage (e.g., UniProt.search(), KEGG.get_pathway_by_gene()). The backticks are markdown code fence delimiters, not shell execution operators. FALSE POSITIVE - documentation only.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 350 locations are in .md files as code examples. No executable .rb files exist in the repository.

medium_findings.title: GO category name flagged as dynamic function constructor
medium_findings.description: 'Molecular Function (MF)' text in GO annotation output code flagged as 'Dynamic function constructor'. This is a Gene Ontology category name, not a dynamic code execution pattern. FALSE POSITIVE.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: GO categories Biological Process, Molecular Function, Cellular Component are standard ontology terms, not executable code.

low_findings.title: PDB structure IDs flagged as C2 keywords
low_findings.description: Example PDB structure identifiers (e.g., '1A1U', '1AIE', '1C26') in identifier mapping documentation flagged as C2 command-and-control keywords. These are legitimate Protein Data Bank IDs for known protein structures. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: PDB identifiers are well-documented 4-character codes for protein structures. Context confirms bioinformatics usage.

low_findings.title: Database code labels flagged as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer matched comment text about database codes (e.g., 'UniProtKB_AC-ID', 'KEGG', 'GeneID') as cryptographic algorithm usage. These are plain-text database identifiers in comments and docstrings, not cryptographic operations. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No actual hashing or encryption calls exist in these files. All matches are in comments describing database aliases.

low_findings.title: Progress print statements flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Print/log statements showing analysis progress, mapping statistics, and query results flagged as 'System reconnaissance'. These are standard progress reporting for data analysis scripts. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Print statements show mapping results, pathway counts, and progress bars - expected behavior for a CLI analysis tool.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem write operations for analysis output
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts write analysis results (CSV, SIF, TXT) to user-specified output directories. All file operations write to user-provided paths and only contain analysis results from legitimate bioinformatics database queries. No arbitrary file overwrite or exfiltration risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network connections to bioinformatics databases
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to legitimate bioinformatics web services (UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, EBI). All URLs are hardcoded to official database endpoints. No data exfiltration to external or unknown hosts. This is the core functionality of the bioservices library.

content.user_title: Access 40+ bioinformatics databases with one Python API
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics researchers spend significant time writing custom API clients for each biological database. This skill provides unified Python access to 40+ bioinformatics web services through a single consistent API for cross-database analysis and identifier mapping.
actual_capabilities: Query 40+ bioinformatics databases (UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, Reactome) with one consistent Python API
Convert biological identifiers between databases using UniProt and UniChem mapping services
Retrieve protein sequences, GO annotations, and pathway data programmatically
Discover KEGG pathways, extract KGML relations, and export interaction networks as SIF files
Search compounds across KEGG, ChEBI, and ChEMBL with cross-reference resolution
Query protein-protein interactions via PSICQUIC-compatible services
limitations: Requires internet access; all operations depend on remote web service availability
Subject to API rate limits from bioinformatics providers; production use needs backoff and retry logic
Does not perform local sequence alignment, structure prediction, or statistical analysis
SOAP and WSDL protocol compatibility varies across services and may have connectivity issues

use_cases.title: Cross-database protein analysis workflow
use_cases.description: Run a multi-step protein analysis from UniProt search through BLAST similarity search to KEGG pathway discovery, all within a single Python script.

use_cases.title: Compound cross-referencing across chemical databases
use_cases.description: Map a compound across KEGG, ChEBI, and ChEMBL databases to compile complete identifier and property information for drug discovery pipelines.

use_cases.title: Batch identifier conversion between databases
use_cases.description: Convert hundreds of gene or protein identifiers between database formats for integration into a unified analysis pipeline with automatic chunking and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Search for a protein and retrieve its sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the UniProt database for the human protein ZAP70. Retrieve its UniProt accession, then fetch the FASTA sequence. Display the protein name, gene names, organism, and sequence length.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to find basic protein information and sequence data

prompt_templates.title: Batch identifier mapping with CSV output
prompt_templates.prompt: Read a list of 50 UniProt accessions from a file. Map each one to its corresponding KEGG gene ID using the UniProt mapping service. Save the results as a CSV file with source ID, target ID, and mapping status columns.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to convert many identifiers between databases for downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Cross-database compound search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for the compound 'Aspirin' in KEGG Compound. Retrieve the detailed entry including formula, mass, and pathway information. Map the KEGG ID to ChEMBL using UniChem, then look up molecular properties in ChEMBL.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs comprehensive compound data from multiple chemical databases

prompt_templates.title: KEGG pathway network analysis for an organism
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze all KEGG pathways for Homo sapiens (hsa). For each pathway, extract the number of genes and interaction count. Save a summary CSV with pathway IDs, names, and interaction type distribution. Export all interactions in SIF format for network visualization.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to perform systems-level pathway analysis and network export

output_examples.input: Search UniProt for human ZAP70 kinase
output_examples.output: UniProt search results displayed in tabular format showing the accession (P43403), gene name (ZAP70), organism (Homo sapiens), sequence length (619 aa), and protein name (Tyrosine-protein kinase ZAP-70). FASTA sequence header and first 60 residues are shown.

output_examples.input: Map UniProt ID P43403 to KEGG
output_examples.output: A key-value mapping showing that UniProt accession P43403 maps to KEGG gene hsa:7535. If multiple IDs are provided, each source ID is listed with its corresponding target database IDs and a success or failed status.

best_practices: Set verbose=False when creating service objects to keep output clean and focused on results
Implement rate limiting with time.sleep() between requests to avoid triggering API limits
Wrap database queries in try/except blocks to handle temporary service outages gracefully
anti_patterns: Making separate API calls for each identifier instead of batching them with comma-separated queries
Running BLAST searches without providing a valid email address (NCBI requires this for access)
Using default timeout values for large batch operations that may take longer to complete

faq.question: What databases does bioservices support?
faq.answer: It supports over 40 bioinformatics databases including UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, Reactome, QuickGO, PSICQUIC, NCBI BLAST, UniChem, and ChEBI.

faq.question: Do I need a separate API key for each database?
faq.answer: No. Most services are publicly accessible without authentication. Some services like NCBI BLAST require only an email address for usage tracking.

faq.question: Can bioservices handle batch queries?
faq.answer: Yes. You can batch identifiers using comma-separated strings in UniProt mapping queries. The included batch_id_converter.py script handles automatic chunking for large datasets.

faq.question: What output formats does bioservices support?
faq.answer: Bioservices returns data in the format provided by each service, including TSV, FASTA, XML, JSON-like dictionaries, and SIF network format.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without programming experience?
faq.answer: The skill provides Python code examples and executable scripts, so basic Python knowledge is helpful. The included scripts can be run from the command line with arguments.

faq.question: Is bioservices free to use?
faq.answer: Yes. Bioservices is an open-source Python package (MIT license). The underlying databases may have their own usage policies and rate limits.
